name: Nimitz Encounter
description: The Nimitz Encounter refers to a series of alleged unidentified aerial phenomena (UAP) sightings by U.S. Navy personnel in November 2004, primarily involving the aircraft carrier USS Nimitz and the cruiser USS Princeton, off the coast of Southern California. Proponents claim these encounters provide compelling evidence of advanced extraterrestrial technology. The central event involved pilots observing a smooth, wingless, oblong object, dubbed the 'Tic Tac' due to its shape, performing maneuvers far exceeding the capabilities of known aircraft. Visual confirmation, radar data, and infrared targeting pod footage are cited as evidence. Mainstream scientific and military perspectives acknowledge the events occurred but suggest alternative explanations such as advanced experimental aircraft, atmospheric anomalies, or sensor malfunctions. Skeptics also point to potential misinterpretations of data, pilot error, and the possibility of psychological factors influencing observations. Official reports, including those from the Department of Defense, have been inconclusive, fueling both speculation and continued investigation. The incident gained significant public attention following the release of declassified videos and pilot interviews.
subtitle: Navy pilots' alleged UFO sighting in 2004

claims.claim: The 'Tic Tac' object exhibited flight characteristics impossible for known aircraft.
claims.evidence: Pilot testimonies describing rapid acceleration, instantaneous stops, and lack of visible propulsion.
claims.criticism: Potential misjudgment of speed and distance, optical illusions, or misinterpretation of sensor data. Unaccounted-for classified technology.

claims.claim: Radar data confirms the presence of unidentified objects moving at incredible speeds and performing unusual maneuvers.
claims.evidence: Radar data from the USS Princeton showing objects appearing and disappearing instantaneously, and exceeding known aircraft speeds.
claims.criticism: Possible radar anomalies, signal interference, or misidentification of conventional aircraft or natural phenomena. Sensor malfunctions.

claims.claim: The released FLIR video provides visual evidence of a physical object with no visible means of propulsion.
claims.evidence: Infrared footage showing a seemingly solid object with no wings or exhaust, emitting heat signature, captured by a Super Hornet's ATFLIR pod.
claims.criticism: Video artifacts, sensor glitches, or misidentification of a conventional aircraft at a distance. Image stabilization issues affecting perceived motion.

keyFigures.name: Commander David Fravor
keyFigures.role: One of the primary pilots who witnessed and described the 'Tic Tac' object.

keyFigures.name: Kevin Day
keyFigures.role: Radar operator aboard the USS Princeton who tracked the UAPs.

keyFigures.name: Mick West
keyFigures.role: Skeptical investigator who analyzes the evidence and proposes alternative explanations.

relatedConcepts: To the Stars Academy of Arts & Science (TTSA)
mythologyConnections: Myths of aerial beings and celestial chariots

timeline.event: The series of UAP encounters by the USS Nimitz Carrier Strike Group.
The Pentagon's Advanced Aerospace Threat Identification Program (AATIP) began investigating the incident.
The New York Times published an article revealing the existence of AATIP and the Nimitz Encounter, sparking public interest.
The Pentagon officially released three videos taken by Navy pilots, including the 'FLIR1' video from the Nimitz Encounter.
